text.title: Process Mining and Automation Examples
text.summary: Scored automation assessment example and recommendation threshold interpretation.

example.code:
Process: Accounts Payable — Invoice Matching
Process Overview: - Current Volume: 450 invoices/day - Current FTE: 3.5 - Error Rate: 8% (manual keying errors) - Cycle Time: 4.2 hours average
Automation Readiness Scoring: | Criterion | Score (1-5) | Evidence | |-----------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------| | Volume | 5 | 450/day >> 100/day threshold | | Standardization | 4 | 85% rule-based; 15% exception rate | | Stability | 4 | Process unchanged 14 months | | Digital Input | 3 | 60% EDI (structured), 40% PDF (unstructured)| | Error Rate | 4 | 8% manual error rate drives rework | | ROI Potential | 5 | $210k/year labor; 8-month payback estimated| | Total | 25/30 | |
Recommendation: Partial automation (score 25 = range 18-24 is exceeded) → Full automation for EDI invoices (60%) → Intelligent document processing for PDF invoices (40%) → Human review retained for exceptions (15% of volume)
ROI Estimate: - Estimated savings: $175k/year (FTE reduction 2.5 → 0.5 exception handlers) - Implementation cost: $85k (licensing + integration) - Payback: 5.8 months

antipattern.description: Automating a high-volume process before checking for standardization — discovering mid-project that 40% of cases are exceptions.
antipattern.reason: Volume alone is not a sufficient criterion. A 1,000-transaction/day process with 50% exceptions requires human judgment more than automation; scoring all six criteria prevents this failure.

antipattern.description: Selecting automation vendor before completing the process assessment — vendor locks in architecture before requirements are known.
antipattern.reason: Complete the assessment matrix and determine whether RPA or intelligent automation applies before engaging vendors; the tool selection follows from the process characteristics, not from the vendor relationship.
